title: Is plain chat the best?
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/oRjZ1WQjigs" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript:
One thing I found interesting: I've tried a lot of things — custom GPTs, skills, various AI tools — and I found myself going back to just plain chat sometimes. Even just the auto, no-thinking model for a lot of questions. There's not really a right or wrong answer — some tasks definitely need thinking capabilities or more reasoning. But I guess what I take from this is that sometimes I've tried to automate something before it really needed to be. Like, let's make a custom tool just for this — but really, plain chat would work fine.

I guess it goes back to something I keep running into: do it manually first. That's the key rule I've come up with for automation. If you do something manually, you deeply understand how it works, and so you know what it's going to take to automate it.

I feel like I've jumped to automating everything before it really needed to be. A takeaway for myself: see the repetition maybe three or four times before looking to build a special tool — because I keep doing the exact same thing over and over, and at that point it makes sense to automate. But yeah, I'm probably just a bit too jumpy to automate everything. Anyway, it's a lot of fun.

title: AI Agent Capability Self-Awareness
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/SVwk9nLQECc" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript:
In the age of AI, most ideas take me about one afternoon to accomplish. But the idea of taking a video, uploading it to a folder, and having it automatically show up on my website has taken me two afternoons. Big idea, I guess — apparently in modern calculations.

Anyway, we'll see if this shows up as expected.

I want to take a minute to talk about a video I saw just recently — it's been circulating around. A guy took ChatGPT Voice and asked it to start a stopwatch. Then there was a reaction from Sam Altman. He said he watched the video and said, "Oh, that feature is about a year out" — because ChatGPT Voice had said, "Okay, I'm starting a stopwatch." Then he said, "Okay, stop it." And ChatGPT said, "Okay, you took seven minutes" — but it had only been like 10 seconds. ChatGPT was just making it up. I was able to duplicate the same issue.

The interesting thing: Sam Altman was focused on the missing tool — the stopwatch. The ChatGPT Voice model simply does not have a stopwatch. That's different from how I think about the issue, because I'm in the middle of designing agents as well, and I run into this exact same problem. An agent says, "Oh, you want to do this? Sure, I can do that" — and then goes ahead and pretends like it will. In my case, it was gathering a bunch of information, and then you get to the end and it fails, because the agent didn't know what it was capable of doing.

The way I see it: go in there and tell your model it doesn't have a stopwatch capability yet, so it doesn't accept requests for tools it doesn't actually have.

Anyway, I just thought that was interesting. It's very relevant to what I do — we have to make these agents know what they're capable of so they don't annoy users. User experience is key in designing tools. That's my thoughts for today.

title: Hello World
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/TMyqjOFRVEc" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript:
Hello world. What a technical pun.

The purpose of this video has nothing to do with being fancy or getting views — it has everything to do with proving that an automated workflow of uploading a video to YouTube will work.

We'll see if this shows up on my channel. If it does, then we know the first hello world experiment was successful. That's what this is all about.

My theory is that it should be very easy to upload new videos — I'm going to try to automate the whole process, so it's just a drag into a folder.

Alright, that's it for my hello world video.
